speaker: interviewer
text: Tape 4. March 18, 1997. Interview with Esther Gever. Continue.

speaker: interviewee
text: This incident on the way to the Russian border-- we walked into that house. And the stove was still warm. And there was no one there. We didn't know what to do. It's someone's home. And we felt that we entered into someone else's sanctity. And with that-- if someone would give us permission, we would have felt differently. But we stayed. And we had food. We didn't even want to-- we didn't know. We just took our own food. We ate. As we were eating, there was a knock on the door. Not a banging-- a knock. A knock on the door-- a civilized knock on the door. And we opened the door. It was a German soldier-- a young soldier. And we froze again. We said, uh oh. And my mother, being the only one that really spoke so eloquently German, started speaking to him. And it seems that he was Austrian. He was from Vienna. And he did not live too far away from us. And he said to my mother, don't stay. Go now. Leave. Go into the woods and leave. I wasn't here. I didn't tell you this. And he left.

speaker: interviewee
text: We did what he said. And we continued. It was days-- months-- I don't know. Time was of absolutely-- no kind-- it didn't mean anything to us. Once, on the-- and that we hear a motorcycle. And the man looks at us. And he's a Polish man. And he says, you're Jews? We said, yes. He says, how did you survive? They just killed everyone in the town over there. And I don't remember the name of the town. That was that soldier that saved our lives. If not for him, we probably would have wound up dead right there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there were still days and nights of being-- some farmers that would sell us some food, and some would say get out of here because we'll kill you-- until we did reach the Soviet border.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] I think it was Lwow. It was the town where my mother was born, where she lived when she was two years old. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that? When was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: It must have been Fall. I don't know that date.

speaker: interviewer
text: Fall of 19-- what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was just after the wars-- we did not stay in Poland long after it was occupied.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was when?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just the year.

speaker: interviewee
text: It must have been still '39. I think it was 1939, still. Because we-- it wasn't that long. [SIGHS] We-- my mother had her relatives in Lwow. We-- her uncle was the [? hasen ?] of the big temple in the city. And we didn't have addresses, but we saw a Jewish man. We approached him. My mother asked for my-- for her uncle. And he said, who doesn't know him? Everybody knows him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see Germans in town?

speaker: interviewee
text: We did not. We did not see anyone. We found the family. The uncle was dead. He took us to his children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who took you to the children?

speaker: interviewee
text: That man took us to the children of my mother's uncle.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did the uncle die?

speaker: interviewee
text: Natural death. He was an older man. And we stayed a day or two. And our grand-- my grandparents proceeded to Stanislavov. I have no idea why they went there, what the plan was-- but we were supposed to meet them there. And we did. We met the family, and we went. In Stanislavov, there was no place that we could find-- apartments weren't available. We were directed to a Talmud Torah, which is a religious school. And it was-- it served the purpose for the refugees to stay there. It was filled with people. [BACKGROUND NOISE] And they gave us a corner. And the benches made beds. And we stayed there. My father looked for work. My mother didn't speak Polish. I started to understand a word here and there. My sister was a baby. She was held all the time, which calmed her.

speaker: interviewer
text: So who was Lwow under at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, It was the Russians. It was the Russian-- did I say? No. We crossed the border at Lwow. Lwow was Russian occupied. And so did Stanislavov. That was the Russian-- we were actually, during the time when we crossed, we were euphoric. We were so-- we felt that we were reborn. We made it. But problems-- life was difficult. And work wasn't available. And my father got sick, physically. And it was an incident that-- I didn't speak Polish, and I went to visit a relative that found lodging in a different area. And I took a little walk, and got lost. And no Polish. And it's getting dark. And the dogs are barking. And I'm alone. And I can't find my way back. I sat down on the sidewalk, and resulted to the only thing I could do-- cry.

speaker: interviewee
text: And two young ladies were coming down the road. And I already learned to be-- I was already a little smarter. Polish or not Polish, I got up and I tried to tell them I'm lost. And I said, no polish. And they said, what language I speak? They spoke a few words in German. And they took me to a house, and that was a doctor. The doctor took me in, asked me all kinds of questions, we communicated. I ate-- cake, milk. And I told him that we are refugees. I come from Vienna. My father is very sick. He can't afford a doctor.

speaker: interviewee
text: He took his satchel, his car-- and that only did my father-- my family found back their daughter, but I brought a doctor. It was almost like the hint of a God. He helped him to get well. And my father got a job. We still slept on benches, but we had enough food.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you sleep on benches?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because, if there was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, where? In that school. In that Talmud Torah. We stuck there. That's where-- that's where we stayed.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people were staying there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, there were so many people. There were several rooms connected, and every room was full of people. Loud people, quiet people, nice people, bad people. All a mixture of people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you bathe?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had sheets. We could-- we took a basin, hung up a sheet, and washed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you cook?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a kerosene lamp. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I can still smell that-- that benzine scent.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did they call those lamps?

speaker: interviewee
text: Why, I don't remember. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It will come to me, but later, maybe. Until one day, they decided to register all the refugees-- the Soviets-- and offer us citizenship. Well, most of us said that we are so grateful, but after the war we would like to go back. And we do appreciate their hospitality. And that was it. They said thank you. Until that night, when again banging on the door. But this time it wasn't [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] it's [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], which means pack and come-- go.

speaker: interviewee
text: We tried to reason and tell them why is-- they never answered. They never answered the question. My mother said, what should I do? Should I pack? He said, take with you everything you can. You're going to need it. We didn't have that much. So again, the sheets, with little things that we acquired. We did not have as much as the Polish Jews, because we came from further. We left three times, already, our belongings.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they-- as we walked out, there were trucks through the night. And a lot of people, again. And also arrested-- we were surrounded with rifles. And they took us-- we arrived at the station with the cattle train. And they loaded us up like cattle. It was dark. We couldn't see exactly, until a few hours later, who our neigh-- neighbors were. We were lucky to get to an upper corner in that cattle train.

speaker: interviewer
text: Upper corner meaning what? Describe it.

speaker: interviewee
text: It-- it was a bridge-- what is it called? A shelf, like. Where underneath was one family. There were four corners. And then the height of the wagon was divided into half. And, of course, the upper half had that little window, too. So you could look out while, for weeks, we were being driven-- not knowing where we going.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we had an upper corner with the window. You couldn't sit up, because there wasn't enough space between the roof of the wagon and-- I think I could, but I don't think someone taller could do that.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was we had to have a hamper and a sheet, which was supposed to be our ladies room-- mens room. We were hungry. We were thirsty. It took a lot of time before [SIGHS] we were given something to eat. Children cried. Old people moaned. Some were sick. Not knowing what is your next step, and why are we here-- we thought that they were going to drive-- take us back to Germany. Every rumor's going around. Everybody knew something, and nobody knew anything. People were just talking. We had a visit from the cousins. Yeah, when we already drove back to Lwow to pick up more people. There were people constantly that were not arrested-- Jews-- came to the wagons to look for their relatives. And sure enough, my mother's cousins were there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which ones?

speaker: interviewee
text: The ones from Lwow that we met when we came to the Russian border.

speaker: interviewer
text: Their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. They'd felt very sorry for us that we are being arrested. In the long run, we were lucky. They all got killed. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Of course, it took four weeks. We-- I learned how to jump on the train while it started to move. I learned the first word-- [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us what [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] is.

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] is hot water from the engine. That is the Russian chai tea. [LAUGHS] It was-- we--

speaker: interviewer
text: Is this is really tea?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. But you use your imagination. And we were given the rations sometimes on time, and sometimes past time. Old bread-- whatever a station had to offer. There was-- elected one-- how you should-- how should I say that? Like a Brigadier, that was-- would be conducting the order of the day. Who is going to bring the food? And sometimes, they would tell us that we have two hours, and we would get off the train-- but constantly guarded. Even deep into Russia, we were still guarded. We were padlocked. When the train-- before the train started moving, we were padlocked. That's why I was lucky sometimes not to be left back, because I stayed till the last possible moment out of that cattle train. And people were talking-- the discussions-- hope and despair at the same time. Broken dreams.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any of the discussions?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh-- Jews always politicize. Always--

speaker: interviewer
text: What were they saying?

speaker: interviewee
text: --politics. Communism. Pro and con. Nazis and why Hitler succeeded. I don't exactly remember, but there were heated discussions. Sometimes even very ugly, angry fights over it. But then everything straightened out, because we still had to survive together until we arrived in a Russian town called [? Arsenault. ?] It was Siberia. It was cold. But that wasn't the end. They put us on a boat-- into a boat. The boat was-- we were like sardines in that boat.

speaker: interviewer
text: On what body of water were we on that boat?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember that either. Was it July-- July-- I don't.

speaker: interviewer
text: On what body of water was the boat?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's what I'm saying. Was it July? I-- sounds like it. I don't remember. Maybe if I think about it, I-- we were three days in that boat. The cattle train was a luxury already against that ride.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of a boat was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was like a fishing boat, stuffed with people. There was just enough place to sit down one next to the other. You could either sit down or get up, but you could not lie down. Dirty, hungry, smelly-- the moisture even-- that stench was terrible. Then we came to a place that had two long barracks and woods. Sky, woods, and those two barracks. We were not right away let in. We were registered. We were given assigned places in the barrack. It was one long barrack for everyone. It was not divided into little rooms. It was-- they called it [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you translate that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That is communal living. My mother was got sick. My mother got very, very sick. There was a doctor. And he didn't know what was wrong with her. She had puss coming out of her body-- an opening that never closed on her waist line. No one knew what it was. She deteriorated from day to day. When this stopped, her breasts started doing the same thing. They took her to a hospital. But there was no hospital in [? Abkasha, ?] but there was a hospital Chet. Hospital-- if you want to call it--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was Chet the nearest town?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was, I think, about 10 or 20 miles away.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you spell that, do you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Chet? C-H-E-T. That's how I would spell it, I think. She-- there was a Polish doctor. There was a Russian doctor. And we were told that she has [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], which means bone tuberculosis. I think that if we were in any other place, maybe they could have cured her. But there was no medicine. There was no food. We were hungry all the time. And she was lost. She was-- didn't have her family, although my grandparents treated her well. She was so sick. My father worked in the woods, chopping trees. My grandparents took care of us. And I was the only one that made daily excursions to Chet to see my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you go there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Alone. Following a road-- a path in the forest-- in the woods.

speaker: interviewer
text: You walked?

speaker: interviewee
text: I walked. Over there, there was bread cheaper on the black market than in [? Abkasha ?] where we were. What I'd learned to do-- I would buy several breads, come back, sell it, and I had the earned bread the same time. I was frightened. It was hard to walk back, because it was heavy. If I heard something in the woods, I was afraid it's a bear. But I kept on going. Sometimes a horse and buggy would pass by and give me a lift. But most of the time it didn't happen. My mother's health became worse and worse, and they finally sent her back to us. They said they can't do anything about it. She was never complained. Never. She was such a good person. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Then, another thing they thought of-- my grandparents. They collected all the older people and said that they have to be separated. And if you could sign that they will not be burdened, then they can stay. My father was going to sign, and they said you can't, because you have a sick wife and a family. We will not accept your signature.

speaker: interviewer
text: What would that signature say I--

speaker: interviewee
text: To-- to have our grandparents with us-- not to separate them. They were sent to an old age home.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did the signature signify-- that you could do what?

speaker: interviewee
text: That they will not fall burden to the government-- that we can support them. And they decided that we could not, because my mother is sick.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were they sent to?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember the place. But we were getting, on birch bark, little cards written from them. I never saw them again. They died there. [SOBBING] I became the care taker of the family. It was hard to handle my father. He wasn't very compassionate anymore. My little sister was-- was pathetic. I was the only one that could keep us alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: We have to stop now.
